prompt: According to Philippians 1, who are listed with Paul in the greeting?
choices_json: Timothy
Titus
Silas
Luke
correct_answer: Timothy
explanation: Paul and Timothy are named together in the greeting of the letter (Philippians 1:1).

prompt: According to Philippians 1, in what does Paul say the Philippians shared from the first day until now?
choices_json: The furtherance of the gospel
The service of the temple
The customs of Jerusalem
The authority of Rome
correct_answer: The furtherance of the gospel
explanation: Paul thanks God for their partnership in furtherance of the gospel from the first day until now (Philippians 1:5).

prompt: According to Philippians 1, who will complete the good work begun in believers?
choices_json: God
Paul
The elders
The saints themselves
correct_answer: God
explanation: Paul is confident that he who began a good work in them will complete it until the day of Jesus Christ (Philippians 1:6).

prompt: According to Philippians 1, what does Paul say happened to his bonds?
choices_json: They became obvious in Christ throughout the whole praetorian guard
They were removed at once
They caused the churches to fall away
They were hidden from everyone
correct_answer: They became obvious in Christ throughout the whole praetorian guard
explanation: Paul says his bonds became revealed in Christ throughout the whole praetorian guard and to all the rest (Philippians 1:13).

prompt: According to Philippians 1, what is Christ to Paul in life and in death?
choices_json: To live is Christ, and to die is gain
To live is duty, and to die is rest
To live is the law, and to die is reward
To live is service, and to die is silence
correct_answer: To live is Christ, and to die is gain
explanation: Paul says, 'For to me to live is Christ, and to die is gain' (Philippians 1:21).

prompt: According to Philippians 1, in what manner should believers conduct themselves?
choices_json: Worthily of the gospel of Christ
According to Roman custom
As disciples of Moses only
With fear of every opponent
correct_answer: Worthily of the gospel of Christ
explanation: Paul says their conduct should be worthy of the gospel of Christ (Philippians 1:27).

prompt: According to Philippians 1, what has been granted to believers on behalf of Christ?
choices_json: Not only to believe in him, but also to suffer for his sake
To reign over nations immediately
To avoid every trial
To keep the old covenant ordinances
correct_answer: Not only to believe in him, but also to suffer for his sake
explanation: Paul says it has been granted to them not only to believe in Christ, but also to suffer on his behalf (Philippians 1:29).

prompt: How does Paul say he remembers the Philippians in Philippians 1?
choices_json: With joy in every request for them
With grief and uncertainty
With fear of their weakness
With silence before God
correct_answer: With joy in every request for them
explanation: Paul says that in every prayer for them all he makes his requests with joy (Philippians 1:4).

prompt: What does Paul pray may abound more and more in Philippians 1?
choices_json: Their love in knowledge and all discernment
Their wealth in earthly goods
Their influence in Rome
Their confidence in ritual obedience
correct_answer: Their love in knowledge and all discernment
explanation: Paul prays that their love may abound more and more in knowledge and all discernment (Philippians 1:9).

prompt: What does Paul say some were doing out of envy and strife in Philippians 1?
choices_json: Preaching Christ
Abandoning the faith
Serving Caesar
Persecuting the saints in Jerusalem
correct_answer: Preaching Christ
explanation: Paul says some indeed preach Christ even out of envy and strife, though others do so from good will (Philippians 1:15).

prompt: What does Paul say will happen through the Philippians' prayers and the supply of the Spirit?
choices_json: This will turn out to his salvation
The churches will be destroyed
His bonds will become meaningless
Christ will no longer be preached
correct_answer: This will turn out to his salvation
explanation: Paul says he knows that this will turn out to his salvation through their prayers and the supply of the Spirit of Jesus Christ (Philippians 1:19).

prompt: What two possibilities does Paul weigh for himself in Philippians 1?
choices_json: Departing to be with Christ or remaining in the flesh
Going to Jerusalem or going to Rome
Returning to Judaism or staying with Christ
Silence or prophecy
correct_answer: Departing to be with Christ or remaining in the flesh
explanation: Paul speaks of the desire to depart and be with Christ, yet also of remaining in the flesh for the Philippians' sake (Philippians 1:23-24).

prompt: How are the Philippians to stand according to Philippians 1:27?
choices_json: Firm in one spirit, with one soul striving for the faith of the gospel
Separately, each guarding his own opinion
Quietly without any public witness
Under fear of their opponents
correct_answer: Firm in one spirit, with one soul striving for the faith of the gospel
explanation: Paul wants to hear that they stand firm in one spirit, with one soul striving for the faith of the gospel (Philippians 1:27).

prompt: What does Paul say their lack of terror before opponents is to them?
choices_json: A sign of destruction to the opponents and of salvation to believers
A sign that conflict should always be avoided
A sign that suffering has no purpose
A sign that Paul will be released immediately
correct_answer: A sign of destruction to the opponents and of salvation to believers
explanation: Paul says their fearless stance is a sign of destruction for opponents and salvation for believers, and that from God (Philippians 1:28).

prompt: Why does Paul rejoice even when some preach Christ from impure motives?
choices_json: Because Christ is proclaimed, and that remains his central concern
Because motives do not matter at all to God
Because envy and strife are forms of holiness
Because he no longer cares about the churches
correct_answer: Because Christ is proclaimed, and that remains his central concern
explanation: Paul says whether in pretense or in truth, Christ is proclaimed, and in this he rejoices (Philippians 1:18).

prompt: What does Philippians 1 show about the purpose of Paul's imprisonment?
choices_json: God used it to advance the gospel and strengthen other believers' boldness
It stopped the spread of the gospel entirely
It proved Paul's ministry had failed
It turned the churches away from Christ
correct_answer: God used it to advance the gospel and strengthen other believers' boldness
explanation: Paul says his circumstances turned out for the progress of the gospel and caused many brothers to speak the word more boldly (Philippians 1:12-14).

prompt: Why does Paul say remaining in the flesh is more necessary in Philippians 1?
choices_json: His continued life would serve the Philippians' progress and joy in the faith
He feared death more than suffering
He was uncertain about being with Christ
He wanted to avoid all hardship
correct_answer: His continued life would serve the Philippians' progress and joy in the faith
explanation: Paul says remaining in the flesh is more needful for them because it would help their progress and joy in the faith (Philippians 1:24-25).

prompt: What does Philippians 1 imply about Christian suffering?
choices_json: Suffering for Christ can be a granted privilege, not merely an unfortunate burden
Suffering proves that faith is absent
Only apostles are ever called to suffer
Believers should measure spiritual life by the absence of trials
correct_answer: Suffering for Christ can be a granted privilege, not merely an unfortunate burden
explanation: Paul says it has been granted to believers not only to believe in Christ but also to suffer for his sake (Philippians 1:29).

prompt: What is the relationship between love and discernment in Philippians 1?
choices_json: Christian love is meant to grow with moral and spiritual insight, not without understanding
Discernment limits love until it disappears
Love and knowledge are opposed in the Christian life
Paul values love but rejects clear judgment
correct_answer: Christian love is meant to grow with moral and spiritual insight, not without understanding
explanation: Paul prays that their love would abound in knowledge and all discernment so they may approve what is excellent (Philippians 1:9-10).

prompt: What does Paul mean by saying his earnest expectation is that Christ will be magnified in his body?
choices_json: Paul wants his whole life and possible death to display Christ's worth publicly
Paul seeks honor for his own spiritual discipline
Paul hopes to escape every bodily hardship
Paul thinks Christ needs human greatness to be exalted
correct_answer: Paul wants his whole life and possible death to display Christ's worth publicly
explanation: Paul says Christ will be magnified in his body, whether by life or by death, showing his desire that everything about him point to Christ (Philippians 1:20).

prompt: Why is standing in one spirit essential in Philippians 1?
choices_json: The gospel calls the church into shared courage and common striving rather than isolated discipleship
Unity matters less than individual boldness
Paul wants the church to avoid all public witness
Only leaders need to contend for the faith
correct_answer: The gospel calls the church into shared courage and common striving rather than isolated discipleship
explanation: Paul describes the church standing firm in one spirit and striving together with one soul for the faith of the gospel (Philippians 1:27).

prompt: How does Philippians 1 connect joy with hardship rather than with comfort?
choices_json: Paul's joy rests in Christ and gospel progress, not in favorable circumstances
Joy appears only when suffering has ended
Hardship automatically destroys Christian joy
Paul rejoices because prison is pleasant
correct_answer: Paul's joy rests in Christ and gospel progress, not in favorable circumstances
explanation: Paul rejoices in prayer, in the proclamation of Christ, and in how his imprisonment serves the gospel, showing joy rooted in Christ rather than comfort (Philippians 1:4, 18, 12-14).

prompt: What does Philippians 1 suggest about the relationship between death and fellowship with Christ?
choices_json: Death is gain because it brings a nearer presence with Christ for the believer
Death is neutral because Christ is absent from it
Death is better only because life has no value
Paul doubts whether Christ is with believers after death
correct_answer: Death is gain because it brings a nearer presence with Christ for the believer
explanation: Paul says to depart and be with Christ is far better, showing the deep fellowship he expects beyond death (Philippians 1:21, 23).

prompt: Why does Paul pray for discernment before speaking of purity and fruit?
choices_json: Right moral fruit requires rightly formed loves and clear spiritual judgment
Purity can grow without any need for understanding
Discernment replaces righteousness in Christ
Fruit matters less than correct slogans
correct_answer: Right moral fruit requires rightly formed loves and clear spiritual judgment
explanation: Paul prays for abounding love with knowledge and discernment so the Philippians may approve what is excellent and be filled with the fruit of righteousness (Philippians 1:9-11).

prompt: How does Philippians 1 portray gospel partnership?
choices_json: It is a shared participation in grace, witness, and endurance centered on Christ
It is mainly financial support without spiritual union
It belongs only to apostles and preachers
It is secondary to individual spirituality
correct_answer: It is a shared participation in grace, witness, and endurance centered on Christ
explanation: Paul ties their fellowship in the gospel to shared grace with him in bonds and in the defense and confirmation of the gospel (Philippians 1:5, 7).

prompt: What is the force of Paul's confidence that God will complete his work in the Philippians?
choices_json: Christian perseverance finally depends on God's faithful action rather than on human strength alone
Believers have no need to pursue holiness
The day of Christ is irrelevant to Christian hope
Paul is only offering sentimental encouragement
correct_answer: Christian perseverance finally depends on God's faithful action rather than on human strength alone
explanation: Paul grounds his confidence in the God who began the good work and will complete it until the day of Jesus Christ (Philippians 1:6).

prompt: How does Philippians 1 hold together individual salvation and corporate witness?
choices_json: Personal faith in Christ is inseparable from the church's united striving for the gospel
Salvation is purely private and unrelated to community life
Corporate witness matters more than personal faith
Paul separates church unity from gospel identity
correct_answer: Personal faith in Christ is inseparable from the church's united striving for the gospel
explanation: Paul speaks of belief in Christ and then immediately calls the church to one-minded striving for the faith of the gospel (Philippians 1:27-29).

prompt: Why does Paul interpret opposition as a sign in Philippians 1?
choices_json: The church's steadfast courage reveals God's saving work in believers and exposes the final end of opposition
Opposition proves believers should compromise the gospel
Every conflict is automatically a sign of failure
Paul wants the Philippians to trust their enemies more than Christ
correct_answer: The church's steadfast courage reveals God's saving work in believers and exposes the final end of opposition
explanation: Paul says their fearless conduct is a sign of destruction to opponents and salvation to believers, and that from God (Philippians 1:28).

prompt: How does Philippians 1 redefine success in ministry?
choices_json: Success is measured by Christ being honored and the gospel advancing, even through suffering and mixed motives
Success means avoiding imprisonment and criticism
Success is primarily numerical growth without hardship
Success depends on the purity of every preacher's motives before Christ can work
correct_answer: Success is measured by Christ being honored and the gospel advancing, even through suffering and mixed motives
explanation: Paul sees his chains serving gospel progress and rejoices that Christ is proclaimed, showing a Christ-centered measure of ministry (Philippians 1:12-18, 20).

prompt: What theological vision of life and death stands behind 'to live is Christ, and to die is gain'?
choices_json: Union with Christ gives meaning to present life and hope to death, so neither circumstance can defeat the believer
Paul believes earthly life is worthless in every respect
Death is gain only because suffering is unbearable
Christ matters only after death, not in present life
correct_answer: Union with Christ gives meaning to present life and hope to death, so neither circumstance can defeat the believer
explanation: Paul's statement reflects a life defined by Christ and a death made gain through being with Christ (Philippians 1:21-23).

prompt: How does Philippians 1 weave together providence, prayer, and the Spirit?
choices_json: God's providential use of Paul's circumstances works through the prayers of the church and the Spirit's supply
Providence makes prayer and the Spirit unnecessary
Prayer replaces God's providence entirely
The Spirit acts independently of the church's prayers
correct_answer: God's providential use of Paul's circumstances works through the prayers of the church and the Spirit's supply
explanation: Paul describes his imprisonment serving gospel advance and says his present situation will turn out through their prayers and the Spirit of Jesus Christ (Philippians 1:12-19).

prompt: Why is Paul's prayer for fruit 'through Jesus Christ' theologically significant in Philippians 1?
choices_json: Even righteous fruit is traced back to Christ as its source and to God's glory as its goal
Fruit of righteousness arises mainly from natural virtue
Paul believes holiness can be detached from Christ
The goal of righteousness is human self-respect
correct_answer: Even righteous fruit is traced back to Christ as its source and to God's glory as its goal
explanation: Paul prays that they may be filled with the fruit of righteousness that comes through Jesus Christ to the glory and praise of God (Philippians 1:11).

prompt: How does Philippians 1 present the church as a public sign in a hostile world?
choices_json: The church's unified, fearless faith becomes a visible testimony to both salvation and judgment
The church should avoid public faithfulness whenever opposition appears
Fear is the best witness to hostile powers
The church's witness belongs only to private meetings
correct_answer: The church's unified, fearless faith becomes a visible testimony to both salvation and judgment
explanation: Paul treats the Philippians' steadfastness and lack of fear before opponents as a sign with theological meaning for both sides (Philippians 1:27-28).

prompt: What does Philippians 1 imply about Christian joy in relation to eschatology?
choices_json: Joy is sustained by the certainty of Christ's day and the believer's future with him
Joy depends only on present success and comfort
Future hope weakens present faithfulness
Paul excludes the day of Christ from practical discipleship
correct_answer: Joy is sustained by the certainty of Christ's day and the believer's future with him
explanation: Paul frames his prayer and confidence with the day of Christ and with the prospect of being with Christ, showing joy rooted in future hope (Philippians 1:6, 10, 23).

prompt: How does Philippians 1 present Christian courage as a theological act rather than mere temperament?
choices_json: Courage arises from participation in the gospel and confidence that belief and suffering are gifts from God
Courage is simply a natural personality trait some possess
Fearlessness matters only for apostles under arrest
Christian courage depends mainly on political support
correct_answer: Courage arises from participation in the gospel and confidence that belief and suffering are gifts from God
explanation: Paul links steadfastness, shared struggle, and suffering for Christ with God's gracious gift, framing courage theologically rather than psychologically (Philippians 1:27-30).

prompt: According to Philippians 2, what should believers do in humility?
choices_json: Count others better than themselves
Seek their own interests first
Avoid serving the saints
Compete for the highest place
correct_answer: Count others better than themselves
explanation: Paul says in humility each should count others better than himself (Philippians 2:3).

prompt: According to Philippians 2, whose mind should be in believers?
choices_json: The mind of Christ Jesus
The mind of Caesar
The mind of Moses
The mind of the philosophers
correct_answer: The mind of Christ Jesus
explanation: Paul says, 'Have this in your mind, which was also in Christ Jesus' (Philippians 2:5).

prompt: According to Philippians 2, to what point did Christ become obedient?
choices_json: To the death of the cross
To the law of Rome
To the temple sacrifices
To the voice of angels
correct_answer: To the death of the cross
explanation: Paul says Christ became obedient even to death, yes, the death of the cross (Philippians 2:8).

prompt: According to Philippians 2, what should believers do without murmurings and disputes?
choices_json: All things
Only acts of worship
Only acts of giving
Only difficult tasks
correct_answer: All things
explanation: Paul says believers should do all things without murmurings and disputes (Philippians 2:14).

prompt: According to Philippians 2, who will Paul send to Philippi soon?
choices_json: Timothy
Luke
Silas
Barnabas
correct_answer: Timothy
explanation: Paul says he hopes in the Lord Jesus to send Timothy shortly to them (Philippians 2:19).

prompt: According to Philippians 2, who had been sick nearly to death?
choices_json: Epaphroditus
Timothy
Paul
Euodia
correct_answer: Epaphroditus
explanation: Paul says Epaphroditus was sick nearly to death, but God had mercy on him (Philippians 2:27).

prompt: According to Philippians 2, for whose work did Epaphroditus come near to death?
choices_json: The work of Christ
The work of the law
The work of Caesar
The work of the synagogue
correct_answer: The work of Christ
explanation: Paul says Epaphroditus came near to death for the work of Christ (Philippians 2:30).

prompt: What does Paul say will make his joy full in Philippians 2?
choices_json: That they think the same way, having the same love, being of one accord
That they all visit Rome
That they avoid every conflict by silence
That they become famous among the churches
correct_answer: That they think the same way, having the same love, being of one accord
explanation: Paul says his joy will be made full when they think the same way and share the same love and purpose (Philippians 2:2).

prompt: What did Christ not consider a prize to be equal with according to Philippians 2?
choices_json: God
Adam
Caesar
The angels
correct_answer: God
explanation: Paul says Christ, existing in the form of God, did not consider equality with God a thing to be grasped (Philippians 2:6).

prompt: What did Christ take upon himself according to Philippians 2?
choices_json: The form of a servant
The throne of David
The armor of Rome
The yoke of Caesar
correct_answer: The form of a servant
explanation: Paul says Christ emptied himself, taking the form of a servant and being made in the likeness of men (Philippians 2:7).

prompt: What does Paul tell believers to work out with fear and trembling?
choices_json: Their own salvation
Their Roman citizenship
Their priestly duty
Their public reputation
correct_answer: Their own salvation
explanation: Paul says to work out their own salvation with fear and trembling (Philippians 2:12).

prompt: Why can believers work out their salvation according to Philippians 2?
choices_json: Because God works in them both to will and to work for his good pleasure
Because human effort alone secures holiness
Because suffering has ended
Because the law now guarantees obedience
correct_answer: Because God works in them both to will and to work for his good pleasure
explanation: Paul immediately grounds the command by saying God is at work in them both to will and to work for his good pleasure (Philippians 2:13).

prompt: How does Paul describe Timothy's concern for the Philippians?
choices_json: He will genuinely care for their state
He seeks only his own comfort
He is unsure whether he knows them
He avoids all church concerns
correct_answer: He will genuinely care for their state
explanation: Paul says he has no one like-minded who will genuinely care for their state as Timothy will (Philippians 2:20).

prompt: How should the Philippians receive Epaphroditus according to Philippians 2?
choices_json: With all joy in the Lord and with honor
With suspicion because of his sickness
As a stranger from another church
With silence and caution
correct_answer: With all joy in the Lord and with honor
explanation: Paul tells them to receive Epaphroditus in the Lord with all joy and to hold such men in honor (Philippians 2:29).

prompt: Why does Paul place Christ's self-emptying at the center of his appeal for unity?
choices_json: True Christian unity is shaped by the humble pattern of Christ himself
Doctrinal truth is less important than example
Paul wants believers to deny Christ's divine status
Humility is optional as long as peace remains outward
correct_answer: True Christian unity is shaped by the humble pattern of Christ himself
explanation: Paul moves from exhorting humility and mutual concern directly into the example of Christ's self-humbling obedience (Philippians 2:1-8).

prompt: What does Philippians 2 teach about the relationship between divine action and human obedience?
choices_json: Believers actively obey because God is actively at work within them
God's work removes the need for effort
Human obedience replaces God's action
Fear and trembling contradict grace
correct_answer: Believers actively obey because God is actively at work within them
explanation: Paul commands believers to work out their salvation and then explains that God works in them to will and to work (Philippians 2:12-13).

prompt: Why does Paul want the Philippians to be blameless and harmless in Philippians 2?
choices_json: So they may shine as lights in a crooked and perverse generation
So they may avoid all contact with unbelievers
So they may boast in their own righteousness
So they may replace Paul's ministry
correct_answer: So they may shine as lights in a crooked and perverse generation
explanation: Paul calls them to be blameless and harmless, children of God without defect, shining as lights in the world (Philippians 2:15).

prompt: What does Paul imply by saying every knee will bow and every tongue confess Jesus as Lord?
choices_json: Christ's humiliation leads to universal acknowledgment of his exalted lordship
Only believers will ever recognize Christ's authority
The Father and Son are in rivalry for honor
Christ's lordship is limited to earth alone
correct_answer: Christ's humiliation leads to universal acknowledgment of his exalted lordship
explanation: Because of Christ's obedience, God highly exalted him and decreed universal homage and confession to his lordship (Philippians 2:8-11).

prompt: Why are Timothy and Epaphroditus included in Philippians 2 after the Christ hymn?
choices_json: They provide living examples of self-giving service shaped by the same mind seen in Christ
Paul needs to change to travel details with no connection
They are presented as rivals to Christ's example
Paul wants to minimize the importance of humility
correct_answer: They provide living examples of self-giving service shaped by the same mind seen in Christ
explanation: After presenting Christ's humility, Paul commends Timothy's genuine concern and Epaphroditus's sacrificial service, embodying the same pattern (Philippians 2:19-30).

prompt: What does Philippians 2 suggest about joy and sacrifice?
choices_json: Paul can rejoice even if poured out in service because gospel sacrifice is joined to Christ's life
Sacrifice cancels joy entirely
Joy belongs only to those free from hardship
Service is valuable only if it preserves comfort
correct_answer: Paul can rejoice even if poured out in service because gospel sacrifice is joined to Christ's life
explanation: Paul says even if he is poured out as a drink offering on the sacrifice and service of their faith, he rejoices (Philippians 2:17).

prompt: How does Philippians 2 portray greatness?
choices_json: Greatness is found in self-humbling obedience and service under God
Greatness belongs to those who seize status aggressively
Greatness is measured by independence from others
Greatness is mainly public recognition before Rome
correct_answer: Greatness is found in self-humbling obedience and service under God
explanation: Christ's path moves through humility and obedience to exaltation, and Paul calls believers to that same selfless mindset (Philippians 2:3-8).

prompt: How does Philippians 2 connect Christ's humility with the church's daily relationships?
choices_json: The self-giving mindset of Christ becomes the pattern for unity, lowliness, and mutual concern among believers
Christ's humility is too unique to shape church life
The chapter separates doctrine from ethics entirely
Only leaders are called to imitate Christ's mind
correct_answer: The self-giving mindset of Christ becomes the pattern for unity, lowliness, and mutual concern among believers
explanation: Paul moves directly from calling for one mind and humility into the example of Christ, showing the practical shape of Christian fellowship (Philippians 2:2-8).

prompt: Why does Paul emphasize that Jesus is exalted 'to the glory of God the Father'?
choices_json: Christ's lordship does not compete with the Father but reveals the Father's glory
The Father reluctantly shares glory with the Son
The Son is exalted apart from the Father's purpose
Jesus is Lord only in a lesser sense than God
correct_answer: Christ's lordship does not compete with the Father but reveals the Father's glory
explanation: Paul says every tongue will confess Jesus Christ as Lord to the glory of God the Father (Philippians 2:11).

prompt: What is the significance of the Philippians shining as lights while holding forth the word of life?
choices_json: Christian holiness and gospel witness belong together in a dark world
Believers should choose either witness or holiness, but not both
The word of life replaces the need for visible conduct
Light refers only to private inward assurance
correct_answer: Christian holiness and gospel witness belong together in a dark world
explanation: Paul connects blameless conduct with shining as lights and holding forth the word of life, showing life and message together (Philippians 2:15-16).

prompt: How does Philippians 2 present Christian leadership through Timothy and Epaphroditus?
choices_json: Leadership is marked by genuine concern, sacrificial service, and willingness to risk oneself for Christ's work
Leadership is defined mainly by status and recognition
Leaders are those who avoid hardship most effectively
Paul honors them only because of their travel usefulness
correct_answer: Leadership is marked by genuine concern, sacrificial service, and willingness to risk oneself for Christ's work
explanation: Paul highlights Timothy's genuine concern and Epaphroditus's nearness to death for Christ's work as worthy patterns for honor (Philippians 2:20-22, 29-30).

prompt: How does Philippians 2 hold together reverence and assurance in obedience?
choices_json: Believers obey with fear and trembling while resting in the fact that God is at work in them
Fear cancels confidence in God's grace
Assurance eliminates the need for reverent obedience
Paul teaches effort without any divine help
correct_answer: Believers obey with fear and trembling while resting in the fact that God is at work in them
explanation: Paul combines the call to work out salvation with fear and trembling and the assurance that God works within believers (Philippians 2:12-13).

prompt: Why is the path from humiliation to exaltation central in Philippians 2?
choices_json: It reveals God's pattern for Christ and shapes the church's understanding of faithful discipleship
It means believers should seek suffering as an end in itself
It applies only to Jesus and has no ethical meaning
Exaltation comes before obedience in God's kingdom
correct_answer: It reveals God's pattern for Christ and shapes the church's understanding of faithful discipleship
explanation: Paul presents Christ's self-humbling and God's exaltation of him as the model and ground for the believers' own humble life together (Philippians 2:5-11).

prompt: What does Philippians 2 imply about God's pleasure in believers?
choices_json: God's good pleasure is actively at work shaping both the willing and the doing of his people
God's pleasure depends on human effort preceding grace
God takes pleasure only in outward success
Believers cannot know anything of God's pleasure now
correct_answer: God's good pleasure is actively at work shaping both the willing and the doing of his people
explanation: Paul says God works in believers both to will and to work for his good pleasure (Philippians 2:13).

prompt: How does Philippians 2 present Christ's lordship in relation to his humility?
choices_json: Christ's universal lordship is revealed through the path of self-emptying obedience rather than self-assertion
Christ became Lord only after ceasing to serve others
Humility and lordship are opposed in the chapter
The cross delays rather than reveals Christ's true status
correct_answer: Christ's universal lordship is revealed through the path of self-emptying obedience rather than self-assertion
explanation: Paul presents Christ's humility unto death and then his exaltation to the name above every name, joining lordship and self-giving obedience (Philippians 2:6-11).

prompt: Why is the Christ hymn in Philippians 2 foundational for Christian ethics?
choices_json: The moral life of the church is grounded in the identity, action, and pattern of Christ himself
Ethics can be built without any doctrine about Christ
The hymn exists only for liturgical beauty and not instruction
Paul uses Christ merely as an unreachable contrast
correct_answer: The moral life of the church is grounded in the identity, action, and pattern of Christ himself
explanation: Paul introduces the hymn precisely to answer how believers should think and act toward one another in humility and love (Philippians 2:2-8).

prompt: How does Philippians 2 guard against both pride and passivity?
choices_json: Believers are called to humble service and active obedience, yet all of it is sustained by God's working within them
Humility means refusing all action and initiative
God's work makes human obedience irrelevant
Service is valuable only if it is publicly recognized
correct_answer: Believers are called to humble service and active obedience, yet all of it is sustained by God's working within them
explanation: The chapter combines Christlike humility, working out salvation, and God's inward work, keeping obedience active yet grace-grounded (Philippians 2:3-5, 12-13).

prompt: What does Philippians 2 suggest about the church's witness before the world?
choices_json: A humble, obedient, united people become lights in darkness by visibly embodying the life of Christ
Witness depends mainly on political influence
Only public preaching counts as witness
The world is changed by argument alone
correct_answer: A humble, obedient, united people become lights in darkness by visibly embodying the life of Christ
explanation: Paul calls them to blameless obedience, shining as lights in the world while holding forth the word of life (Philippians 2:14-16).

prompt: Why are Timothy and Epaphroditus theologically important in Philippians 2 rather than merely biographical details?
choices_json: They show that the self-giving pattern of Christ is meant to be embodied in ordinary Christian ministry and friendship
They are included only to preserve historical information
Their examples overshadow Christ's own example
Paul includes them because doctrine alone is unimportant
correct_answer: They show that the self-giving pattern of Christ is meant to be embodied in ordinary Christian ministry and friendship
explanation: After the model of Christ, Paul commends Timothy's genuine care and Epaphroditus's sacrificial labor as concrete expressions of that same mindset (Philippians 2:19-30).

prompt: How does Philippians 2 unite worship and confession with mission and community life?
choices_json: The confession that Jesus is Lord shapes humility, service, endurance, and the church's public life together
Confession is purely future and has no present ethical force
Worship matters, but community life does not
Mission replaces the need for shared humility
correct_answer: The confession that Jesus is Lord shapes humility, service, endurance, and the church's public life together
explanation: Paul moves from the universal confession of Jesus' lordship back into commands about humble obedience, shining witness, and sacrificial service (Philippians 2:11-16, 17-30).

prompt: What larger biblical theme is echoed when God highly exalts the one who humbled himself?
choices_json: God overturns human pride by exalting the obedient servant rather than the self-exalting ruler
God exalts whoever grasps power first
Humility delays honor but never relates to it
Exaltation belongs only to angels in Scripture
correct_answer: God overturns human pride by exalting the obedient servant rather than the self-exalting ruler
explanation: Philippians 2 presents the Father exalting the Son precisely because of his self-humbling obedience, reflecting God's consistent opposition to pride and honor of humility (Philippians 2:8-9).

prompt: According to Philippians 3, in what should the Philippians rejoice?
choices_json: The Lord
Their own righteousness
Their Roman standing
Their outward circumcision
correct_answer: The Lord
explanation: Paul says, 'Finally, my brothers, rejoice in the Lord' (Philippians 3:1).

prompt: According to Philippians 3, who does Paul warn against three times?
choices_json: Dogs
Romans
Sadducees
False prophets in Asia
correct_answer: Dogs
explanation: Paul says, 'Beware of the dogs, beware of the evil workers, beware of the false circumcision' (Philippians 3:2).

prompt: According to Philippians 3, who are the true circumcision?
choices_json: Those who worship by the Spirit of God and glory in Christ Jesus
Those who rely on the flesh
Those who serve Rome's emperor
Those who boast in temple ritual
correct_answer: Those who worship by the Spirit of God and glory in Christ Jesus
explanation: Paul says believers are the circumcision, who worship by the Spirit of God and glory in Christ Jesus and have no confidence in the flesh (Philippians 3:3).

prompt: According to Philippians 3, what tribe was Paul from?
choices_json: Benjamin
Judah
Levi
Ephraim
correct_answer: Benjamin
explanation: Paul says he was of the tribe of Benjamin (Philippians 3:5).

prompt: According to Philippians 3, what did Paul once count as gain?
choices_json: Things of the flesh such as his privileges and status
Only his imprisonments
His suffering for Christ alone
The gospel among the nations
correct_answer: Things of the flesh such as his privileges and status
explanation: Paul lists his fleshly credentials and says whatever things were gain to him, he counted as loss for Christ (Philippians 3:4-7).

prompt: According to Philippians 3, where is the believers' citizenship?
choices_json: In heaven
In Jerusalem only
In Rome
In the wilderness
correct_answer: In heaven
explanation: Paul says our citizenship is in heaven, from where we also wait for a Savior, the Lord Jesus Christ (Philippians 3:20).

prompt: According to Philippians 3, what will Christ transform?
choices_json: The body of our humiliation
The law of Moses
The throne of Caesar
The temple sacrifices
correct_answer: The body of our humiliation
explanation: Paul says Christ will transform the body of our humiliation to be conformed to the body of his glory (Philippians 3:21).

prompt: What was Paul according to the law as a Pharisee?
choices_json: Blameless in outward righteousness
Weak in observance
Unknown among his people
Opposed to the traditions
correct_answer: Blameless in outward righteousness
explanation: Paul says as to the righteousness which is in the law, he was found blameless (Philippians 3:6).

prompt: What does Paul say he wants to know in Philippians 3?
choices_json: Christ and the power of his resurrection
The secrets of Rome
The names of all the tribes
The full meaning of every vision
correct_answer: Christ and the power of his resurrection
explanation: Paul says he wants to know Christ, the power of his resurrection, and the fellowship of his sufferings (Philippians 3:10).

prompt: What does Paul say he does with the things behind him?
choices_json: Forgets them and reaches forward
Trusts in them for righteousness
Publicly boasts in them
Returns to them for safety
correct_answer: Forgets them and reaches forward
explanation: Paul says forgetting the things which are behind, he stretches forward to the things which are before (Philippians 3:13).

prompt: Toward what does Paul press in Philippians 3?
choices_json: The goal for the prize of the high calling of God in Christ Jesus
The throne of Israel
The praise of men
The traditions of his fathers
correct_answer: The goal for the prize of the high calling of God in Christ Jesus
explanation: Paul says he presses on toward the goal for the prize of the high calling of God in Christ Jesus (Philippians 3:14).

prompt: How does Paul describe many who walk as enemies of the cross of Christ?
choices_json: Their end is destruction, their god is the belly, and their glory is in their shame
They are weak but harmless teachers
They seek only the good of the saints
They live with minds set on heaven
correct_answer: Their end is destruction, their god is the belly, and their glory is in their shame
explanation: Paul weeps over those whose end is destruction, whose god is the belly, whose glory is in their shame, and who mind earthly things (Philippians 3:18-19).

prompt: What kind of righteousness does Paul seek in Philippians 3?
choices_json: The righteousness from God by faith
The righteousness from lawkeeping
The righteousness of the Pharisees
The righteousness of Roman virtue
correct_answer: The righteousness from God by faith
explanation: Paul wants to be found in Christ, not having his own righteousness from the law, but that which is through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God by faith (Philippians 3:9).

prompt: What does Paul say one thing he does in Philippians 3?
choices_json: Forgetting what is behind and reaching forward to what is ahead
Trusting in the flesh when necessary
Waiting without pressing onward
Returning to the righteousness of the law
correct_answer: Forgetting what is behind and reaching forward to what is ahead
explanation: Paul says one thing he does is forget what lies behind and reach forward to what lies ahead (Philippians 3:13).

prompt: Why does Paul count his former gains as loss in Philippians 3?
choices_json: Because the surpassing worth of knowing Christ makes fleshly confidence worthless by comparison
Because those things were sinful in every possible sense
Because he forgot his former life completely
Because earthly privileges cannot exist under grace
correct_answer: Because the surpassing worth of knowing Christ makes fleshly confidence worthless by comparison
explanation: Paul says he counts all things loss for the excellency of the knowledge of Christ Jesus his Lord (Philippians 3:7-8).

prompt: What does Paul mean by wanting to be found in Christ?
choices_json: His standing before God is to rest in Christ's righteousness received by faith, not his own law-based merit
He wants to disappear from public life
He seeks mystical union without ethical consequences
He rejects bodily resurrection in favor of a spiritual ideal
correct_answer: His standing before God is to rest in Christ's righteousness received by faith, not his own law-based merit
explanation: Paul contrasts his own righteousness from the law with the righteousness from God by faith in Christ (Philippians 3:9).

prompt: Why does Paul speak of sharing Christ's sufferings in Philippians 3?
choices_json: Knowing Christ includes conformity to his pattern of suffering and resurrection hope
Suffering itself earns resurrection life
Paul prefers pain to fellowship with Christ
The resurrection can be known without union with Christ
correct_answer: Knowing Christ includes conformity to his pattern of suffering and resurrection hope
explanation: Paul wants to know Christ, the power of his resurrection, and the fellowship of his sufferings, becoming conformed to his death (Philippians 3:10-11).

prompt: What does Philippians 3 teach about spiritual maturity?
choices_json: Maturity does not mean arrival but pressing forward under God's continuing guidance
The mature have no need for further growth
Maturity consists in confidence in religious pedigree
Paul says only apostles can be mature
correct_answer: Maturity does not mean arrival but pressing forward under God's continuing guidance
explanation: Paul says he has not already obtained the goal but presses on, and then tells the mature to have this same mind, trusting God to reveal what is needed (Philippians 3:12-15).

prompt: How does heavenly citizenship shape life in Philippians 3?
choices_json: Believers live with their identity and hope oriented toward Christ's return rather than earthly-minded living
Citizenship in heaven removes concern for bodily resurrection
Heavenly citizenship means escape from present obedience
Paul contrasts heaven only with Rome, not with all earthly-mindedness
correct_answer: Believers live with their identity and hope oriented toward Christ's return rather than earthly-minded living
explanation: Paul contrasts those who mind earthly things with believers whose citizenship is in heaven and who wait for the Savior (Philippians 3:19-20).

prompt: Why does Paul call believers to imitate him and others who walk by the same pattern?
choices_json: Embodied examples help the church discern faithful life over against destructive alternatives
Paul wants personal devotion that excludes Christ
Patterned discipleship is unnecessary in Christian life
Only Paul's past credentials make him worth imitating
correct_answer: Embodied examples help the church discern faithful life over against destructive alternatives
explanation: Paul urges them to imitate him and observe those walking by that pattern, especially because many walk as enemies of the cross (Philippians 3:17-19).

prompt: What does Philippians 3 reveal about confidence in the flesh?
choices_json: Even the strongest religious pedigree cannot rival the worth of Christ as a basis for righteousness
Confidence in the flesh is harmless if one also believes in Christ
Paul's pedigree remained his main boast after conversion
Fleshly confidence applies only to Gentiles
correct_answer: Even the strongest religious pedigree cannot rival the worth of Christ as a basis for righteousness
explanation: Paul piles up his credentials precisely to show that even he must abandon them as grounds for confidence before God (Philippians 3:4-9).

prompt: How does Philippians 3 connect justification and sanctification?
choices_json: Paul receives righteousness by faith and then presses forward in living conformity to Christ
Sanctification replaces the need for righteousness by faith
Justification makes pursuit of holiness unnecessary
Paul separates knowing Christ from moral transformation
correct_answer: Paul receives righteousness by faith and then presses forward in living conformity to Christ
explanation: Paul seeks righteousness from God by faith and then speaks of knowing Christ, sharing his sufferings, and pressing on toward the goal (Philippians 3:9-14).

prompt: Why does Paul combine warning against false confidence with longing for resurrection glory in Philippians 3?
choices_json: Only by losing confidence in the flesh can believers gain the true future found in Christ
Resurrection glory comes through religious status
Paul treats warning and hope as unrelated subjects
Future glory belongs mainly to those with Jewish pedigree
correct_answer: Only by losing confidence in the flesh can believers gain the true future found in Christ
explanation: Paul rejects fleshly gain and then describes knowing Christ, attaining resurrection, and waiting for bodily transformation from the Savior (Philippians 3:7-11, 20-21).

prompt: What is the force of Paul's statement that he has not already been made perfect?
choices_json: Christian maturity is marked by humble pursuit rather than complacent self-satisfaction
Perfection is impossible and therefore not worth pursuing
Paul doubts the truth of the gospel
Only the Philippians are called to grow
correct_answer: Christian maturity is marked by humble pursuit rather than complacent self-satisfaction
explanation: Paul says he has not already obtained or been made perfect, but presses on because Christ Jesus laid hold of him (Philippians 3:12).

prompt: How does Philippians 3 portray the Christian life as both present pursuit and future hope?
choices_json: Believers press on now in Christ while waiting for the Savior who will transform them at his coming
The future removes the need for present striving
Present striving replaces all need for future hope
Paul speaks only of inward transformation, not bodily hope
correct_answer: Believers press on now in Christ while waiting for the Savior who will transform them at his coming
explanation: Paul speaks of pressing toward the prize now and then of waiting from heaven for Christ to transform the body of humiliation (Philippians 3:14, 20-21).

prompt: What does Philippians 3 imply about the church's discernment of teachers and examples?
choices_json: The church must distinguish between patterns of life shaped by the cross and patterns ruled by earthly desire
Examples are irrelevant if doctrine sounds correct
All leaders are equally safe to imitate
Cross-shaped living matters less than rhetorical skill
correct_answer: The church must distinguish between patterns of life shaped by the cross and patterns ruled by earthly desire
explanation: Paul tells them to watch those who walk by the apostolic pattern and warns of others whose lives show hostility to the cross (Philippians 3:17-19).

prompt: How does Philippians 3 relate resurrection to present discipleship?
choices_json: Hope of resurrection shapes present pursuit, suffering, and conformity to Christ
Resurrection is future only and has no effect on present life
Present discipleship earns resurrection as wages
Paul cares only about the soul, not the body
correct_answer: Hope of resurrection shapes present pursuit, suffering, and conformity to Christ
explanation: Paul seeks the resurrection while describing present fellowship in Christ's sufferings and a life of pressing on toward the goal (Philippians 3:10-14).

prompt: Why does Paul contrast earthly-minded enemies of the cross with heavenly citizenship?
choices_json: He wants the church's desires, loyalties, and future hope to be shaped by Christ rather than by appetite and shame
He means believers should despise creation itself
Paul rejects all concern for earthly obedience
Citizenship in heaven abolishes bodily resurrection
correct_answer: He wants the church's desires, loyalties, and future hope to be shaped by Christ rather than by appetite and shame
explanation: Paul contrasts those whose god is the belly and who mind earthly things with believers who await the Savior from heaven (Philippians 3:19-20).

prompt: How does Philippians 3 redefine covenant identity over against fleshly markers?
choices_json: True covenant identity is found in Spirit-wrought worship, boasting in Christ, and righteousness by faith rather than outward credentials
Covenant identity depends mainly on tribe and circumcision
Paul rejects every form of Jewish identity as evil in itself
Faith in Christ adds to fleshly confidence without replacing it
correct_answer: True covenant identity is found in Spirit-wrought worship, boasting in Christ, and righteousness by faith rather than outward credentials
explanation: Paul says believers are the true circumcision and contrasts that with confidence in fleshly credentials, which he counts loss for Christ (Philippians 3:3-9).

prompt: Why is Paul's language of gain and loss theologically important in Philippians 3?
choices_json: It shows conversion as a radical revaluation in which Christ becomes the supreme treasure over every former advantage
It means created goods have no value at all
It treats the gospel mainly as a financial metaphor with little spiritual meaning
Paul wants believers to despise all memory of their past
correct_answer: It shows conversion as a radical revaluation in which Christ becomes the supreme treasure over every former advantage
explanation: Paul deliberately takes what once counted as gain and recasts it as loss because of the surpassing value of Christ (Philippians 3:7-8).

prompt: What does Philippians 3 imply about the shape of Christian hope?
choices_json: Christian hope is bodily, Christ-centered, and bound to the transforming power of the risen Lord
Hope concerns only escape from the body forever
The future is secured through human religious achievement
Paul offers only vague spiritual optimism
correct_answer: Christian hope is bodily, Christ-centered, and bound to the transforming power of the risen Lord
explanation: Paul says believers await the Savior from heaven, who will transform their lowly body to be like his glorious body by his powerful working (Philippians 3:20-21).

prompt: How does Philippians 3 keep the pursuit of holiness from turning back into legalism?
choices_json: The whole pursuit flows from having been laid hold of by Christ and from receiving righteousness by faith
Paul replaces grace with a new law of striving
Holiness is pursued without any relation to Christ's prior work
Pressing on means returning to confidence in the flesh
correct_answer: The whole pursuit flows from having been laid hold of by Christ and from receiving righteousness by faith
explanation: Paul presses on not to earn Christ but because Christ Jesus laid hold of him, and he seeks righteousness from God by faith (Philippians 3:9, 12).

prompt: Why does Paul speak with tears about enemies of the cross in Philippians 3?
choices_json: His warning is pastoral and grieving, not merely polemical, because false ways of life destroy people
Paul enjoys condemning opponents publicly
Tears mean he is uncertain about the gospel
He weeps because heavenly citizenship has no present meaning
correct_answer: His warning is pastoral and grieving, not merely polemical, because false ways of life destroy people
explanation: Paul says he has told them often and now says even weeping that many walk as enemies of the cross, showing deep pastoral sorrow (Philippians 3:18).

prompt: How does Philippians 3 portray the cross as a dividing line in human life?
choices_json: The cross separates those who glory in Christ and live by resurrection hope from those ruled by earthly appetite and shame
The cross is important only for Paul's private spirituality
Everyone relates to the cross in the same way regardless of life direction
The cross abolishes the need for discernment about how one walks
correct_answer: The cross separates those who glory in Christ and live by resurrection hope from those ruled by earthly appetite and shame
explanation: Paul contrasts those who reject the way of the cross with believers who count all loss for Christ and await transformation from him (Philippians 3:8-11, 18-21).

prompt: What deeper point lies behind Paul's refusal to call himself already perfected?
choices_json: Union with Christ creates confident striving, not self-congratulation, until final resurrection completeness
Paul believes growth in grace is impossible
Perfection belongs only to angels and never to redeemed humanity
The Christian life has no future goal beyond initial conversion
correct_answer: Union with Christ creates confident striving, not self-congratulation, until final resurrection completeness
explanation: Paul presses on because Christ has laid hold of him, yet he admits he has not already attained the fullness toward which he moves (Philippians 3:12-14).

prompt: According to Philippians 4, whom does Paul urge to be of the same mind in the Lord?
choices_json: Euodia and Syntyche
Lydia and Chloe
Priscilla and Phoebe
Lois and Eunice
correct_answer: Euodia and Syntyche
explanation: Paul urges Euodia and Syntyche to think the same way in the Lord (Philippians 4:2).

prompt: According to Philippians 4, in whom should believers always rejoice?
choices_json: The Lord
Themselves
Their leaders
Their circumstances
correct_answer: The Lord
explanation: Paul says, 'Rejoice in the Lord always' (Philippians 4:4).

prompt: According to Philippians 4, what should be known to all men?
choices_json: Your gentleness
Your learning
Your Roman status
Your self-confidence
correct_answer: Your gentleness
explanation: Paul says, 'Let your gentleness be known to all men' (Philippians 4:5).

prompt: According to Philippians 4, what will guard believers' hearts and thoughts in Christ Jesus?
choices_json: The peace of God
The strength of Rome
Their own discipline alone
The wisdom of philosophers
correct_answer: The peace of God
explanation: Paul says the peace of God will guard their hearts and thoughts in Christ Jesus (Philippians 4:7).

prompt: According to Philippians 4, what has Paul learned in whatever state he is in?
choices_json: To be content
To seek more influence
To avoid all need
To mistrust his friends
correct_answer: To be content
explanation: Paul says he has learned in whatever state he is in to be content (Philippians 4:11).

prompt: According to Philippians 4, through whom can Paul do all things?
choices_json: Through Christ who strengthens him
Through his own willpower
Through Caesar's favor
Through the help of every church
correct_answer: Through Christ who strengthens him
explanation: Paul says, 'I can do all things through Christ, who strengthens me' (Philippians 4:13).

prompt: According to Philippians 4, whose household especially sends greetings?
choices_json: Caesar's household
The household of Annas
The house of Lydia
The house of Aquila
correct_answer: Caesar's household
explanation: Paul says all the saints greet them, especially those of Caesar's household (Philippians 4:22).

prompt: How does Paul say believers should make their requests known to God?
choices_json: By prayer and supplication with thanksgiving
By anxiety and haste
By silence without asking
By appealing first to Rome
correct_answer: By prayer and supplication with thanksgiving
explanation: Paul tells them to present their requests to God by prayer and supplication with thanksgiving (Philippians 4:6).

prompt: What kinds of things does Paul tell believers to think about in Philippians 4?
choices_json: Things that are true, honorable, just, pure, lovely, and of good report
Things that stir envy and rivalry
Things that preserve fear of opponents
Things that magnify earthly status
correct_answer: Things that are true, honorable, just, pure, lovely, and of good report
explanation: Paul lists true, honorable, just, pure, lovely, and commendable things as the focus of believers' thought (Philippians 4:8).

prompt: What does Paul say the Philippians did well in Philippians 4?
choices_json: They had fellowship with his affliction
They avoided every cost of gospel work
They forgot him in prison
They kept their gifts for themselves
correct_answer: They had fellowship with his affliction
explanation: Paul says they did well to have fellowship with his affliction (Philippians 4:14).

prompt: What kind of offering does Paul call the Philippians' gift?
choices_json: A sweet-smelling fragrance, an acceptable sacrifice, well pleasing to God
A legal obligation under the old covenant
A burden that Paul resented receiving
A political tribute to Rome
correct_answer: A sweet-smelling fragrance, an acceptable sacrifice, well pleasing to God
explanation: Paul describes their gift with sacrificial language as a fragrant offering pleasing to God (Philippians 4:18).

prompt: How will God supply the Philippians' need according to Philippians 4?
choices_json: According to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus
According to Rome's treasury
According to their own strength
According to the law of exchange
correct_answer: According to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus
explanation: Paul says God will supply every need according to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus (Philippians 4:19).

prompt: What does Paul say he has learned how to do both in Philippians 4?
choices_json: To be abased and to abound
To conquer and to retreat
To preach and to remain silent
To labor and to stop serving
correct_answer: To be abased and to abound
explanation: Paul says he knows how to be abased and also how to abound (Philippians 4:12).

prompt: When did the Philippians alone have fellowship with Paul in giving and receiving?
choices_json: In the beginning of the gospel when he departed from Macedonia
Only after he arrived in Rome
At the council in Jerusalem
When he first met Timothy
correct_answer: In the beginning of the gospel when he departed from Macedonia
explanation: Paul says that in the beginning of the gospel, when he departed from Macedonia, no assembly had fellowship with him in giving and receiving but the Philippians only (Philippians 4:15).

prompt: What does Philippians 4 teach about anxiety and prayer?
choices_json: Anxiety is answered not by denial but by prayerful dependence and thanksgiving before God
Prayer matters only after anxiety has disappeared
Anxiety is solved mainly by self-mastery
Thanksgiving is optional when asking God for help
correct_answer: Anxiety is answered not by denial but by prayerful dependence and thanksgiving before God
explanation: Paul commands them not to be anxious but to bring everything to God in prayer with thanksgiving, promising God's peace (Philippians 4:6-7).

prompt: Why does Paul pair right thinking with right practice in Philippians 4?
choices_json: The Christian life involves both disciplined thought and embodied obedience under God's presence
Thought matters, but practice does not
Practice alone can replace truth
The mind should remain detached from discipleship
correct_answer: The Christian life involves both disciplined thought and embodied obedience under God's presence
explanation: Paul tells believers to think on what is excellent and then to do what they learned and saw in him, promising the God of peace will be with them (Philippians 4:8-9).

prompt: What does Paul's contentment in Philippians 4 reveal about Christian strength?
choices_json: True strength is Christ-given steadiness in abundance and need alike
Contentment means passivity and lack of effort
Strength is measured by material comfort
Paul's contentment came from having no real needs
correct_answer: True strength is Christ-given steadiness in abundance and need alike
explanation: Paul says he has learned contentment in every circumstance and can do all things through Christ who strengthens him (Philippians 4:11-13).

prompt: Why does Paul say he seeks not the gift but the fruit that increases to the Philippians' account?
choices_json: He values the spiritual fruit of their generosity more than personal benefit
He no longer wants their partnership
Financial gifts are unimportant to God
He is ashamed of receiving any help
correct_answer: He values the spiritual fruit of their generosity more than personal benefit
explanation: Paul says he seeks not the gift itself, but the fruit that increases to their account, emphasizing its spiritual value (Philippians 4:17).

prompt: What does Philippians 4 imply about the peace of God?
choices_json: God's peace actively guards the inner life of believers in Christ beyond normal human calculation
Peace means the absence of every external problem
Peace comes only when believers understand every circumstance
Peace belongs only to those without needs
correct_answer: God's peace actively guards the inner life of believers in Christ beyond normal human calculation
explanation: Paul says the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard hearts and thoughts in Christ Jesus (Philippians 4:7).

prompt: How does Philippians 4 portray Christian generosity?
choices_json: Generosity is fellowship in gospel affliction and a sacrifice pleasing to God
Giving is merely practical bookkeeping
Generosity matters only when the church is wealthy
Paul sees gifts as interruptions to ministry
correct_answer: Generosity is fellowship in gospel affliction and a sacrifice pleasing to God
explanation: Paul calls their support fellowship in his affliction and later describes their gift as an acceptable sacrifice well pleasing to God (Philippians 4:14, 18).

prompt: Why is gentleness important in Philippians 4?
choices_json: It reflects a near Lord and a life freed from self-protective anxiety
It is useful only in private prayer
It means never correcting anyone
It applies only to leaders in conflict
correct_answer: It reflects a near Lord and a life freed from self-protective anxiety
explanation: Paul links gentleness with the statement 'The Lord is at hand' and then calls believers away from anxiety to prayerful trust (Philippians 4:5-6).

prompt: How does Philippians 4 connect prayer, peace, and practice?
choices_json: Prayer brings God's peace to guard believers, and obedient practice brings the presence of the God of peace
Peace comes only from right thinking without prayer
Practice matters, but peace and prayer are separate topics
The chapter treats peace as a merely inward emotion
correct_answer: Prayer brings God's peace to guard believers, and obedient practice brings the presence of the God of peace
explanation: Paul first promises the peace of God through prayer and then says the God of peace will be with those who practice what they learned (Philippians 4:6-9).

prompt: What does Philippians 4 suggest about the relationship between rejoicing and hardship?
choices_json: Rejoicing in the Lord is possible even amid need, conflict, and imprisonment because it is anchored in Christ rather than circumstances
Joy is possible only after problems are solved
Hardship and joy are always mutually exclusive
Paul commands joy without giving any reason for it
correct_answer: Rejoicing in the Lord is possible even amid need, conflict, and imprisonment because it is anchored in Christ rather than circumstances
explanation: Paul commands rejoicing while discussing anxiety, need, and his own imprisonment background, showing a joy rooted in the Lord himself (Philippians 4:4, 11-13, 22).

prompt: Why is Paul's language about learning contentment significant in Philippians 4?
choices_json: Contentment is portrayed as a grace-taught discipline of life in Christ, not a natural temperament
Paul was born content and never faced real need
Contentment comes through indifference to people
The chapter teaches contentment without dependence on Christ
correct_answer: Contentment is portrayed as a grace-taught discipline of life in Christ, not a natural temperament
explanation: Paul repeatedly says he has learned contentment and learned the secret of living in many conditions through Christ's strength (Philippians 4:11-13).

prompt: How does Philippians 4 frame material support within the gospel?
choices_json: Material giving is interpreted as gospel partnership, spiritual fruit, and worshipful sacrifice before God
Financial support is treated as morally neutral logistics only
Paul rejects all gifts as distractions from ministry
Only very large gifts have spiritual significance
correct_answer: Material giving is interpreted as gospel partnership, spiritual fruit, and worshipful sacrifice before God
explanation: Paul speaks of the Philippians' support as fellowship, fruit to their account, and a fragrant offering pleasing to God (Philippians 4:14-18).

prompt: How does Philippians 4 portray the nearness of the Lord as practical truth?
choices_json: The Lord's nearness shapes gentleness, steadies prayer, and frees believers from anxious self-protection
The Lord's nearness makes prayer unnecessary
It refers only to a distant future event with no present effect
It encourages passivity rather than action
correct_answer: The Lord's nearness shapes gentleness, steadies prayer, and frees believers from anxious self-protection
explanation: Paul places 'The Lord is at hand' right between the command to let gentleness be known and the call to reject anxiety through prayer (Philippians 4:5-6).

prompt: What does Philippians 4 imply about the relation between thought life and holiness?
choices_json: Holy living requires disciplined attention to what is true and excellent, not mental passivity
The mind is unimportant if actions look correct
Only mystical experiences matter for holiness
Paul treats thought as morally neutral
correct_answer: Holy living requires disciplined attention to what is true and excellent, not mental passivity
explanation: Paul commands believers to think on what is true, honorable, just, pure, lovely, and commendable, linking thought to faithful practice (Philippians 4:8-9).

prompt: How does Philippians 4 connect reconciliation and worship in the life of the church?
choices_json: Agreement in the Lord belongs alongside rejoicing, prayer, and shared worship as part of mature church life
Conflict has no effect on the church's spiritual life
Worship matters more than restored relationships
Paul treats Euodia and Syntyche as unrelated to the chapter's main themes
correct_answer: Agreement in the Lord belongs alongside rejoicing, prayer, and shared worship as part of mature church life
explanation: Paul begins the chapter by urging Euodia and Syntyche to agree in the Lord and then moves into commands about rejoicing, gentleness, prayer, and peace (Philippians 4:2-7).

prompt: How does Philippians 4 present Christian peace as more than emotional calm?
choices_json: Peace is God's active guarding presence over the believer's inner life within prayerful communion with Christ
Peace is merely a self-generated state of mind
Peace means the end of all practical responsibilities
Peace comes only after full understanding of every circumstance
correct_answer: Peace is God's active guarding presence over the believer's inner life within prayerful communion with Christ
explanation: Paul describes the peace of God as guarding hearts and thoughts in Christ Jesus in response to prayer and thanksgiving (Philippians 4:6-7).

prompt: Why is Paul's statement 'I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me' best read in the context of Philippians 4?
choices_json: It concerns Christ-enabled endurance and faithfulness across every circumstance of need and abundance
It promises unlimited success in any chosen ambition
It refers only to miraculous feats in ministry
It means believers never experience weakness
correct_answer: It concerns Christ-enabled endurance and faithfulness across every circumstance of need and abundance
explanation: Paul says this immediately after describing contentment in hunger, fullness, abundance, and need, so the statement concerns Christ's sustaining strength in all conditions (Philippians 4:11-13).

prompt: How does Philippians 4 tie generosity to worship?
choices_json: The Philippians' financial gift becomes priestly language of sacrifice because gospel giving is an act offered to God
Giving is valuable only for its financial efficiency
Worship and generosity belong to separate spheres
Paul rejects sacrificial language after Christ's coming
correct_answer: The Philippians' financial gift becomes priestly language of sacrifice because gospel giving is an act offered to God
explanation: Paul calls their gift a fragrant aroma, an acceptable sacrifice, well pleasing to God, showing generosity as worshipful offering (Philippians 4:18).

prompt: What does Philippians 4 suggest about the connection between God's provision and the believer's generosity?
choices_json: The God who receives generous sacrifice from his people is also the God who richly supplies their need in Christ
Giving forces God into debt to believers
Provision is unrelated to life in Christ
God supplies only material abundance, never spiritual need
correct_answer: The God who receives generous sacrifice from his people is also the God who richly supplies their need in Christ
explanation: After describing their gift as pleasing to God, Paul assures them that his God will supply every need according to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus (Philippians 4:18-19).

prompt: How does Philippians 4 gather the letter's larger theology into its closing chapter?
choices_json: Joy, unity, prayer, peace, Christ-sufficiency, partnership, and grace all reappear as the mature life of the church
The chapter abandons the themes developed earlier in the letter
Only practical advice remains after doctrine ends
The conclusion depends mainly on Caesar's household for its meaning
correct_answer: Joy, unity, prayer, peace, Christ-sufficiency, partnership, and grace all reappear as the mature life of the church
explanation: Philippians 4 revisits joy in the Lord, agreement in the Lord, prayerful peace, Christ's strength, gospel partnership in giving, and grace in the final blessing (Philippians 4:2-4, 6-7, 13-19, 23).

prompt: Why is the greeting from Caesar's household significant in Philippians 4?
choices_json: It quietly testifies that Christ's reign reaches into the very center of imperial power structures
It means the church has become politically dominant in Rome
It proves Paul now serves Caesar rather than Christ
It matters only as a personal travel note
correct_answer: It quietly testifies that Christ's reign reaches into the very center of imperial power structures
explanation: The mention of saints from Caesar's household fittingly closes a letter where imprisonment has served gospel advance, showing Christ's work reaching even the imperial sphere (Philippians 1:12-13; 4:22).

prompt: How does Philippians 4 present Christ-sufficiency without encouraging self-sufficiency?
choices_json: Paul's contentment is real, but it is explicitly sustained by Christ's strength rather than by autonomous inner resources
Contentment means believers no longer need God or others
Paul teaches that spiritual maturity eliminates all dependence
The chapter praises Stoic self-mastery over grace
correct_answer: Paul's contentment is real, but it is explicitly sustained by Christ's strength rather than by autonomous inner resources
explanation: Paul has learned contentment, yet he immediately attributes his ability to endure all circumstances to Christ who strengthens him (Philippians 4:11-13).
